correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute appendicitis
required_evidence: Acute abdominal pain that migrates to the right lower quadrant (or focal RLQ pain) with localized tenderness/guarding
Associated anorexia and/or nausea/vomiting
Low-grade fever and/or systemic symptoms
Leukocytosis or neutrophil predominance (supportive, not required)
No alternative explanation on focused history/exam; urgent surgical evaluation indicated

expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to mildly low if vomiting

expected_lab_results.BUN: Normal to mildly elevated if dehydration

medications: Normal saline 0.9% 1 L IV bolus then maintenance as needed
Ondansetron 4 mg IV every 6-8 hours as needed for nausea
Acetaminophen 1 g PO/IV every 6 hours as needed (max 3 g/day typical adult; adjust per liver disease/age)
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV every 12 hours PLUS Metronidazole 500 mg IV every 8 hours (pre-op antibiotics for suspected appendicitis in penicillin allergy with urticaria)
non_pharmacological: NPO (nothing by mouth)
Urgent surgical consult for appendectomy evaluation
Serial abdominal exams and vital sign monitoring
Avoid laxatives/enemas; avoid NSAIDs if concern for renal injury/dehydration
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate ED/surgical evaluation now; reassess within hours (serial exams) and post-op follow-up typically 1-2 weeks after appendectomy

Antibiotics started to reduce infection risk, chosen to avoid penicillin due to urticarial allergy
Surgical consultation for definitive management (appendectomy) and monitoring for worsening symptoms
Review penicillin allergy details and avoidance plan
Red flags: worsening pain, fever, persistent vomiting, fainting, increasing abdominal rigidity—seek immediate care
Do not eat or drink until evaluated; avoid self-treating with laxatives/enemas

Review labs to support clinical suspicion and rule out key alternatives (e.g., UTI, pregnancy-related pathology), while recognizing labs do not rule appendicitis in/out alone.

State concern for appendicitis based on symptom pattern and exam context; emphasize urgency and risk of perforation; note need for imaging/surgical evaluation rather than outpatient definitive diagnosis.
Critical safety check before prescribing antibiotics/analgesics; confirm penicillin allergy and plan to avoid beta-lactams if urticarial/immediate-type reaction.
Non-critical safety step before prescribing to reduce adverse events (review interactions with current meds, including QT-prolonging agents if antiemetics considered).

Initiate appropriate supportive care orders consistent with appendicitis pathway (NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antiemetic) and start antibiotics selected to avoid penicillin due to urticarial allergy; arrange urgent surgical evaluation.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis concern/urgency, explain treatment plan including allergy-based antibiotic choice, address concerns and red flags, and provide lifestyle precautions (NPO, avoid laxatives/enemas).
If not immediately admitted, ensure reliable observation/follow-up plan and clear return precautions; otherwise document surgical consult/ED transfer plan.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and appropriate escalation to urgent evaluation.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking college student working part-time retail. You came because you can’t get bleeding to stop after a tooth extraction. Start by volunteering: the socket has been oozing on and off since the extraction, you keep tasting blood, you’ve changed gauze many times because it soaks through, and today you feel a bit lightheaded and tired.

If asked about bleeding history, share: you’ve bled longer than others after a dental cleaning and a small cut, you bruise easily, gums sometimes bleed when brushing, and you’ve had nosebleeds that take a long time to stop. Say you’ve never been told you have low platelets and you’re not on blood thinners.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: umbilical stump bleeding as a baby, cuts re-bleeding hours later, or family anxiety after head bumps.

Resist discussing heavy periods and post-sex spotting; only admit with gentle, direct questioning.

Express your misconception: you read it might be a platelet problem and worry your blood can’t clot at all; wonder about a rare inherited disorder. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and voice cost/insurance and missing work/class concerns.

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg 1 tablet PO daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
confounders: Normal PT/INR and aPTT despite clinically significant bleeding (can delay recognition of rare clot-stabilization disorders such as factor XIII deficiency)
Recent dental extraction with local tissue trauma and delayed re-bleeding pattern
Use of combined oral contraceptive (can reduce menstrual bleeding and partially mask baseline bleeding tendency)
Iron deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss may mimic fatigue/pallor from other causes and complicate assessment of bleeding severity

differentials: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital factor XIII deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital FXIII deficiency presenting with delayed post-procedural bleeding
required_evidence: Prolonged bleeding after dental extraction with otherwise normal screening coagulation tests (PT/INR, aPTT, thrombin time, fibrinogen)
Normal platelet count (no thrombocytopenia) and no evidence of platelet-type mucocutaneous bleeding pattern predominance
Clinical pattern consistent with clot instability/delayed bleeding rather than immediate bleeding

Exclusion of differentials using available labs: normal PT argues against congenital prothrombin deficiency; normal aPTT argues against combined factor V and VIII deficiency; normal platelet count and lack of platelet function testing availability makes Glanzmann less supported but should be considered if mucosal bleeding is prominent

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be mildly decreased if bleeding is significant; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: May be mildly decreased if bleeding is significant; otherwise normal

medications: Factor XIII concentrate 40 IU/kg IV once (for active post-extraction bleeding or peri-procedural hemostasis); repeat dosing guided by hematology/clinical response
Tranexamic acid 10-15 mg/kg IV every 8 hours OR 1.3 g PO three times daily for 3-5 days (adjunct for oral/dental bleeding, if no contraindications)
non_pharmacological: Local hemostatic measures at extraction site (direct pressure, suturing, topical hemostatic agents as available)
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs; use acetaminophen for pain
Consult hematology urgently for confirmation testing (FXIII activity/antigen) and to plan ongoing prophylaxis if indicated
If FXIII concentrate unavailable: use cryoprecipitate (contains FXIII) per institutional protocol in consultation with hematology/transfusion medicine
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Same day/within 24 hours reassessment after hemostatic therapy; hematology follow-up within 1 week for definitive testing and long-term management plan

must_include: Bleeding can occur despite normal PT/INR and aPTT because factor XIII stabilizes the clot after it forms
This condition can cause delayed or prolonged bleeding after procedures like dental extractions
Need for confirmatory FXIII activity testing and hematology involvement
Goal is to replace factor XIII to stabilize the clot and stop bleeding
How factor XIII concentrate is given (IV) and that dosing may be repeated based on response/specialist guidance
Role of antifibrinolytic therapy (tranexamic acid) as an adjunct for oral bleeding
Local measures at the dental site are important alongside factor replacement
Review penicillin allergy and avoid prescribing penicillin-class antibiotics unless specifically evaluated
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent care (ongoing bleeding, dizziness/syncope, large bruising, severe headache/neurologic symptoms)
Discuss that future procedures require advance planning with hematology/dentistry for prophylaxis
Avoid medications that increase bleeding risk (aspirin/NSAIDs) unless directed
Inform all healthcare/dental providers of the bleeding disorder
Consider medical alert identification and maintaining an emergency plan for bleeding episodes

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting problem (e.g., post-dental bleeding), timing (delayed/prolonged), severity, hemodynamic/neurologic symptoms, prior bleeding history, prior FXIII deficiency diagnosis/testing, current meds (including NSAIDs/aspirin/anticoagulants), and pregnancy status if relevant.
Required critical safety check before any prescribing; specifically confirm penicillin allergy details and any prior reactions to plasma-derived products or factor concentrates.

Order appropriate initial bleeding evaluation and confirmatory testing pathway: CBC/platelets, PT/INR, aPTT (often normal), fibrinogen; and FXIII activity assay (confirmatory) with hematology involvement.
Review results to support suspected/known congenital FXIII deficiency and rule out alternative causes (thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy).

Non-critical safety check before prescribing adjuncts (e.g., tranexamic acid) and to identify drugs that increase bleeding risk (NSAIDs/antiplatelets/anticoagulants).

Initiate treatment plan consistent with guidelines: factor XIII replacement (FXIII concentrate IV; dosing/repeat dosing per hematology and response) and consider antifibrinolytic therapy (tranexamic acid) as adjunct for oral bleeding; avoid contraindicated meds and avoid penicillin-class antibiotics given allergy unless explicitly evaluated.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis mechanism and delayed bleeding; explain treatment goals, IV FXIII concentrate, adjunct tranexamic acid, and local dental measures; address allergy/urgent warning signs/future prophylaxis planning; lifestyle counseling (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs unless directed, inform providers, medical alert/emergency plan).

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit nocturnal event semiology and sleep context to distinguish sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy from parasomnias; assess frequency, clustering, stereotypy, duration, awareness, injuries, incontinence, post-ictal state, triggers, and witness description.

Assess epilepsy risk factors and differentials: prior seizures, febrile seizures, CNS infection/trauma, family history of SHE/ADNFLE, psychiatric history, OSA symptoms, substance/alcohol use, and medication adherence.

Assess safety and severity: injuries, dangerous behaviors at night, daytime sleepiness, cognitive complaints, mood, suicidality screening (esp. if considering antiseizure meds), and pregnancy/contraception status if relevant.
Order diagnostic confirmation testing when appropriate for L3: video-EEG with PSG (or prolonged video-EEG) to capture events; consider MRI brain epilepsy protocol if not previously done.
Review available study results (if provided by environment) to support diagnosis and exclude mimics (parasomnia, OSA, PNES).

Synthesize history (brief, stereotyped, clustered nocturnal hypermotor events from NREM) and any EEG/PSG data to diagnose sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (ADNFLE/SHE) and document differential/uncertainty if studies pending.

Explain diagnosis milestone: why events fit sleep-related focal seizures from frontal lobe vs nightmares/sleepwalking; NREM timing, brevity, stereotypy, clustering; link cognitive impairment to nocturnal seizure burden/sleep disruption and potential improvement with control.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies (including penicillin allergy details) and prior severe cutaneous adverse reactions to antiseizure meds.

Critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interacting meds (hormonal contraception, anticoagulants, antiretrovirals, other ASMs, macrolides/azole antifungals, grapefruit, etc.) given carbamazepine enzyme induction.

Gather contraindication-relevant history needed for prescribing: pregnancy plans, hepatic disease, hematologic history, hyponatremia risk, cardiac conduction disease, and current sodium-lowering drugs.

Initiate first-line therapy (commonly carbamazepine or related sodium-channel agent) with gradual titration and counseling; document rationale and alternatives if contraindicated or patient preference.

Explain treatment milestone: goals (seizure suppression, improved sleep), titration, common adverse effects, serious warning signs (SJS/TEN features, fever, facial swelling, jaundice), and hyponatremia symptoms prompting evaluation.

Address concerns milestone: penicillin allergy does not preclude carbamazepine but new rash must be reported; enzyme induction reduces efficacy of many meds (notably hormonal contraception/anticoagulants); shared decision-making about confirmatory testing and medication choice.

Lifestyle/safety milestone: sleep hygiene, avoid triggers (sleep deprivation, alcohol, missed doses), nighttime safety and seizure first aid, driving/occupational guidance per local regulations, adherence and follow-up importance.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, tolerability, adherence, review test results, and adjust therapy; provide earlier contact instructions for adverse effects or seizure escalation.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety netting, and plan documentation.

must_include: Diagnosis is a clotting factor problem (low/abnormal factor II) rather than a platelet problem
Why PT/INR is prolonged and how that fits factor II deficiency
Differentials briefly: hemophilia usually prolongs aPTT; Glanzmann affects platelets with normal PT/aPTT; factor XIII deficiency has normal PT/aPTT; combined FV/FVIII typically prolongs aPTT more
Goal is to replace missing clotting activity (PCC preferred; FFP alternative) and control bleeding
Expected benefits and major risks (notably thrombosis risk with PCC; volume overload/transfusion reactions with FFP)
When to seek urgent care (head trauma, heavy bleeding, black/tarry stools, vomiting blood, severe joint swelling/pain)
Review current medication (carisoprodol) for sedation/fall risk and bleeding risk from trauma
Confirm penicillin allergy (hives) and ensure it is documented for any future antibiotic needs
Discuss need for hematology testing/confirmatory factor assays and family screening considerations
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin unless directed; use acetaminophen for pain when appropriate
Inform all clinicians/dentists of bleeding disorder before procedures
Carry medical alert identification and an emergency plan for bleeding episodes

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding history, severity, triggers, prior diagnoses, family history, and current meds/allergies; screen for urgent bleeding (head injury, GI bleed, hemodynamic compromise).

Order initial hemostasis evaluation to support congenital prothrombin deficiency and rule out common alternatives (CBC/platelets, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen; consider mixing study and factor II activity if available).

Interpret labs to confirm pattern consistent with factor II deficiency (typically prolonged PT/INR; aPTT may be normal or mildly prolonged depending on level) and exclude platelet disorders and factor XIII deficiency.
State congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency as working/confirmed diagnosis and document key differentials and rationale.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (factor vs platelet), interpret PT/INR, brief differentials; explain treatment (PCC vs FFP, risks/benefits, urgent care triggers); address concerns (carisoprodol fall risk, penicillin allergy documentation, confirmatory assays/family screening); lifestyle education (avoid NSAIDs/aspirin, inform clinicians/dentists, medical alert plan).
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm and document penicillin allergy (hives) and any other allergies relevant to planned products/meds.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review interactions/sedation risk with carisoprodol and any planned analgesics or adjuncts.

Critical safety check before prescribing; assess contraindications to PCC/FFP (history of thrombosis, DIC, volume overload/heart failure, severe liver disease considerations, current anticoagulant use).

If active significant bleeding/procedure need: prescribe/arrange factor replacement (4-factor PCC preferred; FFP alternative) per institutional protocol; provide safer pain control (acetaminophen) and avoid NSAIDs; ensure urgency/referral as appropriate.
Arrange hematology follow-up for confirmatory factor II activity, inhibitor evaluation if indicated, genetic counseling/family screening, and individualized emergency plan.
Close encounter after safety-netting, follow-up plan, and patient understanding confirmed.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old cooperative elementary school teacher. Speak clearly and calmly, but sound worried and tired. Start by volunteering: you get out of breath very fast even walking across the room, have a persistent dry cough, feel exhausted with no stamina, and climbing one flight of stairs causes chest tightness and you must stop. If asked, add: occasional lightheadedness with exertion, ankle puffiness by day’s end, waking at night short of breath needing to sit up, bluish lips/fingertips when very winded, a few episodes of pink/blood-streaked mucus, and mild unintentional weight loss from fatigue eating. Do not mention unless directly probed: worse at altitude/planes, avoiding activities, needing to sleep propped up, pounding heartbeat with minor effort, and rounded fingertips. Only if trust/rapport is built, admit you’ve avoided sex due to breathlessness and sometimes skip immune-condition meds fearing infections; don’t want judgment. Express misconceptions: you thought asthma or panic; you tried a friend’s inhaler with little help. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open. Ask about costs, insurance, time off work, oxygen, and travel to specialty centers.

medications: Propranolol 20 mg PO twice daily
Mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg PO twice daily
Prednisone 5 mg PO daily
Furosemide 20 mg PO daily as needed for edema
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS 1 tablet PO three times weekly (PJP prophylaxis)
comorbidities: STING-associated vasculopathy with onset in infancy (SAVI)
Secondary erythrocytosis due to chronic hypoxemia
confounders: SAVI-related interstitial lung disease can mimic or coexist with pulmonary hypertension and contribute to hypoxemia
Beta-blocker therapy (propranolol) may blunt tachycardic compensation and worsen exercise intolerance, obscuring severity of right heart failure
Use of pulmonary arterial hypertension vasodilators may precipitate pulmonary edema in PVOD, complicating empiric treatment decisions
Chronic immunosuppression (mycophenolate/prednisone) increases risk of opportunistic infection presenting with dyspnea and hypoxemia
Secondary erythrocytosis can mask anemia and alter interpretation of oxygen delivery and symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pulmonary veno-occlusive disease (PVOD)
acceptable_alternatives: Pulmonary arterial hypertension with suspected PVOD phenotype
Group 1 pulmonary hypertension (idiopathic PAH) with features concerning for PVOD
required_evidence: Severe hypoxemia on room-air arterial blood gas (low PaO2 and SaO2) often out of proportion to symptoms
Evidence of right-heart strain/heart failure biomarker elevation (elevated NT-proBNP) compatible with pulmonary hypertension physiology
Absence of hypercapnia (PaCO2 normal/low) supporting diffusion/vascular limitation rather than hypoventilation
Clinical need to avoid empiric pulmonary vasodilators due to risk of precipitating pulmonary edema when PVOD suspected

expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) pH: Normal to mildly alkalemic (respiratory alkalosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) PaCO2: Low to normal (often low due to hyperventilation)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) HCO3-: Normal to mildly low (compensation if respiratory alkalosis)
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Elevated (suggesting right ventricular strain/heart failure physiology)
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): Normal to mildly elevated (may be elevated with RV strain; should not show a dynamic ACS pattern without other evidence)
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - Hemoglobin: Normal to elevated (possible secondary erythrocytosis if chronic hypoxemia)

medications: Oxygen therapy titrated to maintain SpO2 92-96% (or patient's known baseline if chronic) via nasal cannula or face mask as needed
Furosemide 20-40 mg IV or PO once, then titrate to euvolemia based on symptoms and response
non_pharmacological: Avoid initiation of pulmonary arterial vasodilators (e.g., PDE-5 inhibitors, endothelin receptor antagonists, prostacyclins) unless under expert pulmonary hypertension center supervision due to risk of pulmonary edema in PVOD
Urgent referral to a specialized pulmonary hypertension center for definitive evaluation and transplant assessment (PVOD is often transplant-requiring)
Assess volume status and implement fluid/salt restriction if congestive features present
Monitor closely for pulmonary edema after any change in oxygenation/diuresis strategy; consider inpatient monitoring if significant hypoxemia or RV strain
Counsel to avoid high altitude and strenuous exertion until stabilized; ensure up-to-date vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal) if applicable
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Same day/within 24 hours: pulmonary hypertension specialist evaluation or hospital admission if unstable; otherwise within 1 week with PH center and close monitoring

must_include: PVOD is a rare cause of pulmonary hypertension due to blockage/scarring in small pulmonary veins
It can cause low oxygen levels and strain on the right side of the heart
It behaves differently from typical PAH and requires specialist management
Immediate goals are improving oxygenation and reducing fluid overload if present (oxygen and diuretics)
Certain pulmonary hypertension vasodilator medicines can worsen PVOD by causing pulmonary edema; do not start them without PH expert supervision
Need for urgent referral to a pulmonary hypertension center; transplant evaluation may be part of care
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent care (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, syncope, rapid swelling, pink frothy sputum)
Explain the rationale for avoiding contrast exposure given prior immediate hives and need for alternative imaging strategies or premedication protocols if unavoidable
Review current propranolol use and ensure it is appropriate given symptoms; do not stop abruptly without a plan
Avoid high altitude and heavy exertion until stabilized; pace activities
Adherence to oxygen as prescribed and monitoring home oxygen saturation if available
Salt/fluid guidance if swelling or congestion is present; keep follow-up appointments promptly

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected PVOD/pulmonary hypertension: dyspnea progression, hypoxemia, exertional symptoms, syncope/chest pain, edema/orthopnea, hemoptysis/pink frothy sputum, prior PH workup, exposures (chemo/radiation), connective tissue disease/HIV, family history, pregnancy status if relevant, and current meds including propranolol.

Order baseline evaluation to support PH/PVOD assessment and rule out mimics/complications: CBC (polycythemia/anemia), CMP (renal/hepatic for diuretic planning), BNP/NT-proBNP, TSH as indicated, HIV/autoimmune serologies if risk, ABG or oximetry, and pregnancy test when applicable.
Review results to assess severity (hypoxemia, BNP elevation), end-organ status, and readiness for supportive therapy and referral.

State working diagnosis: suspected PVOD (rare cause of pulmonary hypertension) and emphasize need for specialist confirmation; avoid premature labeling as typical PAH that could lead to unsafe vasodilator initiation.
Critical safety check before ordering any imaging/meds: confirm allergies, especially prior immediate hives to iodinated contrast; document reaction type/timing and alternatives/premedication needs.
Critical safety check before prescribing any new medications, including diuretics or supportive meds; confirm drug allergies and prior reactions.
Critical check before prescribing: review interactions with current meds (including propranolol), OTCs, and supplements; ensure safe supportive regimen.

Initiate supportive care consistent with PVOD safety: oxygen if hypoxemic; consider diuretics if volume overloaded; explicitly do not start PAH vasodilators (e.g., PDE5 inhibitors, endothelin receptor antagonists, prostacyclins) without PH expert supervision due to pulmonary edema risk.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment approach and risks of vasodilators in PVOD, address urgent warning signs and contrast allergy implications, review propranolol use and avoidance of abrupt cessation, and provide lifestyle guidance (altitude/exertion, oxygen adherence, salt/fluid guidance).
Arrange urgent referral to pulmonary hypertension center; discuss transplant evaluation as potential pathway; set short-interval follow-up and escalation plan.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and ensuring referral and follow-up are in place.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sheehan syndrome (postpartum hypopituitarism) with secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism
acceptable_alternatives: Central adrenal insufficiency with central hypothyroidism (suspected pituitary cause)
required_evidence: History compatible with postpartum pituitary infarction (e.g., severe postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension) and subsequent hypopituitarism symptoms (amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea, lactation failure, fatigue, weight changes, low libido)
Low 8 AM serum cortisol with inappropriately low/normal ACTH (secondary adrenal insufficiency pattern)
Low free T4 with inappropriately low/normal TSH (central hypothyroidism)
Low prolactin (often) and low gonadotropins (LH/FSH) with low estradiol (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism)

expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (8 AM): Low (suggestive of adrenal insufficiency; typically <3–5 mcg/dL strongly suggestive; intermediate values require dynamic testing)
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (consistent with secondary adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated despite hypothyroxinemia)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Low or inappropriately normal (often reduced in Sheehan syndrome)

If hypogonadism and no contraindications: Estradiol (transdermal 50-100 mcg/day) with progesterone (micronized progesterone 200 mg PO nightly for 12 days each month) for endometrial protection if uterus present

If patient is currently on testosterone: Discontinue testosterone unless a clear, guideline-supported indication is confirmed; if used for hypoactive sexual desire disorder in postmenopausal women, use the lowest effective transdermal dose and monitor for virilization and serum levels (avoid supraphysiologic dosing)
non_pharmacological: Provide adrenal insufficiency sick-day rules and emergency plan (increase oral hydrocortisone during illness; seek urgent care for vomiting/inability to take PO)
Prescribe/teach use of emergency injectable hydrocortisone and recommend medical alert identification

Assess fertility goals; counsel on reproductive options and need for specialist management if pregnancy desired
Review symptoms of adrenal crisis and hypothyroidism; encourage adherence and regular monitoring
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess symptoms, vitals, and tolerance; repeat free T4 (and clinical review) 4-6 weeks after starting/adjusting levothyroxine; ongoing endocrinology follow-up every 3-6 months until stable

Calorie-controlled diet with dietitian experienced in PWS; emphasize high-fiber, nutrient-dense foods; avoid sugar-sweetened beverages
Daily physical activity plan tailored to hypotonia and fitness; include resistance training as feasible
Behavioral support and caregiver training for food-related behaviors and routines
Screen and treat obstructive sleep apnea before and during growth hormone therapy; monitor for snoring, daytime somnolence
Endocrine monitoring: growth parameters, IGF-1, thyroid function, glucose/A1c; assess pubertal development and consider sex steroid replacement if indicated and safe
Bone health: ensure adequate calcium/vitamin D intake; consider DXA if hypogonadism or fracture risk
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after initiating or adjusting endocrine therapy; then every 3-6 months for metabolic/endocrine monitoring (A1c/glucose, IGF-1, thyroid labs, weight/BMI, blood pressure) and safety surveillance

minimal_paths.description: Evaluate PWS complications, order baseline labs, provide counseling and safety net; defer medications until results available while discussing GH/thyroid/diabetes pathways.
must_collect: sleep symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime somnolence)
diabetes symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue) and prior glucose history
hypothyroid symptoms (fatigue, constipation, cold intolerance) and prior thyroid testing
allergies and reaction details (penicillin rash; morphine severe nausea/vomiting)
renal/hepatic disease history (relevant to metformin safety)
caregiver supports and home food environment
steps: ASK: focused PWS complication screen and medication/allergy history
CHECK_ALLERGY: document penicillin and morphine reactions
ORDER_LAB: A1c/fasting glucose, free T4+TSH, IGF-1 (if GH considered), creatinine/eGFR if metformin may be used
DIAGNOSE: confirm PWS due to point mutation/imprinting center defect and list risk complications
EDUCATE: diagnosis + treatment overview + lifestyle/food security + adverse effects/warning signs + ongoing monitoring
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: timed lab review and referrals (endocrinology/sleep as indicated)

minimal_paths.description: Evaluate, order and review labs, then initiate indicated therapy with required safety checks; provide full education and follow-up plan.

allergies and reaction details (penicillin rash; morphine severe nausea/vomiting)

steps: ASK: focused history including meds, comorbidities, and goals
ORDER_LAB: A1c/fasting glucose, free T4+TSH, IGF-1, creatinine/eGFR (and additional labs as clinically indicated)
GET_RESULTS: interpret results and identify active problems
DIAGNOSE: PWS due to point mutation/imprinting center defect + associated conditions (e.g., central hypothyroidism, prediabetes/diabetes)
CHECK_INTERACTION: review interactions with current meds
PRESCRIBE: levothyroxine if low free T4; metformin if glucose intolerance/diabetes and renal function adequate; document GH therapy referral/monitoring plan if applicable
EDUCATE: required milestones including adverse effects and urgent-care precautions; reinforce food security plan
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: monitoring intervals for thyroid labs, glucose/A1c, IGF-1 and sleep symptoms; caregiver support
